apps.name: OpenMasjid Display
apps.tagline: Run prayer timetables, cameras and HDMI on every screen in your masjid — over your network
apps.author: hasan-ismail
apps.description:
**OpenMasjid Display** turns one small computer into the control room for every
screen in your masjid. Each TV gets its own network video link (RTSP) that you
point a cheap "RTSP decoder" box at once — then you decide, from your phone or a
computer, what each screen shows:

- **Prayer timetables** — beautiful, full-screen prayer clocks calculated on the
  device (no internet needed). Make as many as you like, each with its own colours
  to match the room it hangs in. Shows a large live clock, the Hijri and Gregorian
  dates, every prayer's Adhan **and** Iqamah time, Jumu'ah, and a gentle countdown
  to the next prayer with the current prayer highlighted.
- **Cameras** — bring in any security/IP camera or an imam camera and put it on a
  screen with one tap (great for overflow rooms and the women's section).
- **HDMI sources** — plug a laptop or a recording into an HDMI-to-network encoder
  and send it to the screens you choose.

Set it once and forget it: a weekly **schedule** can switch a screen to a camera
for Jumu'ah, then back to the timetable afterwards — automatically. Volunteers can
always take over instantly from the simple mobile page.

It's designed to be light enough for a Raspberry Pi, a mini-PC, or a Proxmox
container, and the whole control panel matches the calm OpenMasjidOS look in both
light and dark.

**Nothing to fill in here — just install.** When it's running, click **Open**, create
your control-panel password, and set everything up inside: your masjid details,
this server's network address, your screens, cameras and schedules. Each screen
shows you the exact link to paste into its decoder.
apps.compose:
# OpenMasjid Display — two small services that run side by side:
#   • app       — the control panel (web UI + API) and the timetable video renderer
#   • mediamtx   — the RTSP server every screen's decoder connects to
# Both are least-privilege: no privileged mode, host networking, devices or sockets.
#
# There is nothing to configure at install time: install it, open the control
# panel, create your admin password, and set everything up inside the app (saved
# to the data volume).
services:
  app:
    image: ghcr.io/hasan-ismail/openmasjiddisplay:0.2.0
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      - mediamtx
    environment:
      # Internal wiring only (how the app reaches the RTSP server on the private
      # network). All masjid configuration is done in the app and stored in /data.
      PORT: "8080"
      DATA_DIR: /data
      MEDIAMTX_API_URL: http://mediamtx:9997
      MEDIAMTX_RTSP_INTERNAL: rtsp://mediamtx:8554
      MEDIAMTX_RTSP_LOOPBACK: rtsp://127.0.0.1:8554
    ports:
      # host:container — OpenMasjidOS offers "Open" on the lowest published port,
      # so the control panel (7860) sits below the RTSP port (8554). The platform
      # picks a different free host port automatically if one is taken.
      - "7860:8080"
    volumes:
      - data:/data

  mediamtx:
    image: bluenviron/mediamtx:1.19.1
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      # Control API (private network only — not published to the host).
      MTX_API: "yes"
      MTX_APIADDRESS: ":9997"
      # Force RTSP-over-TCP: the most compatible, NAT/firewall-friendly transport
      # for commodity TV decoder boxes (no UDP port range to publish).
      MTX_RTSPTRANSPORTS: "tcp"
      # Trim everything we don't use, to stay light on a Raspberry Pi.
      MTX_RTMP: "no"
      MTX_HLS: "no"
      MTX_WEBRTC: "no"
      MTX_SRT: "no"
      MTX_LOGLEVEL: "info"
    ports:
      - "8554:8554"

volumes:
  data:
